content.problemStatement:
The most important part of a GSM network is so called **Base Transceiver Station (BTS).** These transceivers form the areas called **cells** (this term gave the name to the cellular phone) and every phone connects to the BTS with the strongest signal (in a little simplified view). Of course, BTSes need some attention and technicians need to check their function periodically.

The technicians faced a very interesting problem recently. Given a set of BTSes to visit, they needed to find the shortest path to visit all of the given points and return back to the central company building. Programmers have spent several months studying this problem but with no results. They were unable to find the solution fast enough. After a long time, one of the programmers found this problem in a conference article. Unfortunately, he found that the problem is so called "Traveling Salesman Problem" and it is very hard to solve. If we have NNN BTSes to be visited, we can visit them in any order, giving us N!N!N!  possibilities to examine. The function expressing that number is called factorial and can be computed as a product:

1.2.3.4....N1.2.3.4....N1.2.3.4....N. The number is very high even for a relatively small NNN.

The programmers understood they had no chance to solve the problem. But because they have already received the research grant from the government, they needed to continue with their studies and produce at least **some** results. So they started to study behaviour of the factorial function.

For example, they defined the function ZZZ. For any positive integer NNN, ZZZ(NNN) is the number of zeros at the end of the decimal form of number N!N!N!. They noticed that this function never decreases. If we have two numbers N1<N2N_1 < N_2N1​<N2​ then  Z(N1)≤Z(N2)Z(N_1) \le Z(N_2)Z(N1​)≤Z(N2​). It is because we can never "lose" any
trailing zero by multiplying by any positive number. We can only get new and new zeros. The function ZZZ is very interesting, so we need a computer program that can determine its value efficiently.

Input:There is a single positive integer TTT on the first line of input (equal to about 100000100000100000). It stands for the number of numbers to follow. Then there are TTT lines, each containing exactly one positive integer number NNN, 1≤N≤1091 \le N \le 10^91≤N≤109.

Output:For every number NNN, output a single line containing the single non-negative integer Z(N)Z(N)Z(N).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 100000) – the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the integer Z(N) – the number of trailing zeros in N!.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each N we sum floor(N/5)+floor(N/25)+... . Example: N=60 → 60/5=12, 60/25=2, total 14 zeros.
N=1 → 0 zeros, N=5 → 1 zero, N=10^9 → 249,999,998 zeros by the series.
Applying the division series to the two large numbers yields the shown results.

solution.approach: The number of trailing zeros in N! equals the exponent of 10 in its prime factorisation. Since 10 = 2·5 and there are always more factors 2 than 5, the answer is the total number of factors 5 in the product 1·2·…·N. This can be computed by repeatedly dividing N by 5: Z(N) = ⌊N/5⌋ + ⌊N/25⌋ + ⌊N/125⌋ + … until the divisor exceeds N. Each division is O(1) and the loop runs O(log_5 N) times.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T · log₅ N) – at most about 13 iterations per test case because 5^13 > 10^9.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
